coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as light-responsive oxidase-like nanozyme for colorimetric detection of total antioxidant capacity in fruits
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jinquan
Zhou, Yun
Xiao, Yue
Cai, Shuo
Huang, Changsheng
Guo, Shuo
Sun, Yuanqiang
Song, Rong-Bin
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: Evaluation of total antioxidant capacity (TAC) in fruits is essential for dietary guidance and health monitoring. Here, we have exploited light-response carbon dots (CDs) as oxidase-like nanozyme to determine the TAC of fruits. The CDs possess excellent oxidase-like activity with light stimulation due to the accelerated intramolecular charge transfer caused by abundant electron donating/drawing groups in precursors. The scavenger experiment reveals that the catalytic intermediate could be hydroxyl radical, which can oxidize the colorimetric substrate. With the introduction of antioxidants, the oxidization of colorimetric substrate will be alleviated due to the scavenging of this intermediate by antioxidants. Based on this, we have successfully detected three antioxidants and obtained TAC of fruits with desirable results. This work affords a rapid, cost-effective and convenient analysis tool for TAC, as well as building a strong bridge between CDs and the development of photo-responsive oxidase-like nanozymes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Oxidase
Total antioxidant capacity
Photo-response
Nanozymes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Catalytic mechanism for photo-responsive oxidase-like activity
Detection of antioxidants and TAC in fruit
Colorimetric detection of antioxidant activity of AA, GSH and Cys

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21974125, 22174131), Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Henan Province (22TRTSTHN002), Key Project of Scientific and Technological in Henan Province (212102311059), the 111 Project of Henan Province (CXJD2021001), National 111 Project of China (D20003) and state Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2108).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM images and b) size distributions histogram of the CDs. Scale bar = 10 nm. c) FT-IR spectra of CDs. d) XPS curve. e) XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s. f) The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of CDs.

a) UV–vis absorption spectra of samples containing CDs (with light irradiation), TMB (with light irradiation), CDs + TMB (without light irradiation), CDs + TMB (with light irradiation), respectively. b) Photocontrollable oxidase mimetic activity of CDs. c) Steady-state kinetic assay of CDs by using TMB concentration as variate. d) Lineweaver-Burk plots of light-responsive oxidase-like activity of CDs.

a) TMB oxidation degree by CDs under light irradiation with the addition of different scavengers. TMB oxidation degree was expressed by the ratio of absorbance value with and without scavenger. b) UV–vis absorption spectra of the TMB solution catalyzed by CDs under light irradiation, but in nitrogen or air atmospheres.

a) The UV–vis absorption spectra of catalytic reaction systems in the presence of different concentrations of AA (0, 1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40 and 50 μM) and b) the corresponding calibration curve and linear relation (inset). c) Selectivity of CDs-coupled TMB system towards AA detection in the presence of various metal ions and amino acids. (1: blank, 2–12: Gly, KCl, MgCl2, NaCl, Phe, Try, Tyr, His, Leu, Lys, Thr, 13: AA). d) Histogram of the total antioxidant capacity of three fruit juices.
Schematic illustration of TAC detection using oxidase-like activity of light-responsive CDs.
Detection of the total antioxidant capacity in Fruit Samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as light-responsive oxidase-like nanozyme for colorimetric detection of total antioxidant capacity in fruits

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Evaluation of total antioxidant capacity (TAC) in fruits is essential for dietary guidance and health monitoring. Here, we have exploited light-response carbon dots (CDs) as oxidase-like nanozyme to determine the TAC of fruits. The CDs possess excellent oxidase-like activity with light stimulation due to the accelerated intramolecular charge transfer caused by abundant electron donating/drawing groups in precursors. The scavenger experiment reveals that the catalytic intermediate could be hydroxyl radical, which can oxidize the colorimetric substrate. With the introduction of antioxidants, the oxidization of colorimetric substrate will be alleviated due to the scavenging of this intermediate by antioxidants. Based on this, we have successfully detected three antioxidants and obtained TAC of fruits with desirable results. This work affords a rapid, cost-effective and convenient analysis tool for TAC, as well as building a strong bridge between CDs and the development of photo-responsive oxidase-like nanozymes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous development of society, the increased life pressure and unhealthy living habits have posed serious harm to people’s physical health. The most direct manifestation of this hazard is the excessive generation of free radicals, disrupting redox balance in humans or even increases the incidence of various diseases such as diabetes, inflammation, cardiovascular disease(Pagan et al., 2022; Singh et al., 2015). As a defense system of the human metabolism, antioxidants can scavenge free radicals and effectively prevent cellular organs from oxidative stress and oxidative damage(Forman & Zhang, 2021; Neha et al., 2019; Wang & Kang, 2020). However, most antioxidants cannot be produced by the human organism itself and have to be supplemented from exogenous nutrition like fruits(Sheng et al., 2022; Stedile et al., 2016). In this case, acquiring the quantitative information of antioxidants in these fruits is of growing importance, since it can provide guidance to make reasonable dietary choices for the regulation of oxidative stress.

Considering that the antioxidants in fruits are diversity, the total antioxidant capacity (TAC) has been used as a comprehensive antioxidant indicator to assess the gross activity(Guo et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021). Up to now, various analytical methods has been developed to measure the TAC, which can be summarized for chromatography, spectrometry, and electrochemical categories(Hu et al., 2020; Munteanu & Apetrei, 2021; Plaza et al., 2014). Among them, the spectrometry, especially colorimetry, has been frequently exploited due to the feature of simpleness, convenient, speediness and cost-effectiveness(Bener et al., 2018; Mukdasai et al., 2019). A typical mode to realize the colorimetric assay of TAC is to utilize the competitive mechanism between oxidable chromogenic substrates and antioxidants. To endow this mode with excellent analysis performance, the establishment of powerful oxidation strategy is an essential precondition. Recently, the emergence of nanozymes has provided new opportunities for the design of oxidation process, due to their merits like low cost, good stability and easy preparation. In general, the peroxidase-like nanozymes that can catalyze hydrogen peroxide to implement oxidation have been developed for colorimetric assay of TAC(Cui et al., 2018; Lou et al., 2019; Yadav et al., 2019). However, the disadvantages originated from the introduction of hydrogen peroxide, including volatility, instability and self-decomposition, drive us to optimize these assay systems. On this context, the oxidase-like nanozymes that can catalyze oxygen to realize the oxidation of chromogenic substrates have been adopted to replace the peroxidase-like nanozymes (Ding et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2018). For instance, Fe-Mn bimetal stabilized by dextran has been revealed to manifest oxidase activity and applied for colorimetric assay of TAC(Han et al., 2022). Although the oxidase-like nanozymes are a good candidate, their activities will greatly reduce with long-term exposure to air, as they persistently react with oxygen. In view of this, they are recommended to store in oxygen-free environments or use after preparing them as quickly as possible. To break through these restrictions, one feasible solution is to use the light-responsive oxidase-like nanozymes, whose activities can be initiated only when light stimulation is existed. Therefore, with the intention of simplifying the practical process, the development of analytical platform involved by light-responsive oxidase-like nanozymes for TAC has become an urgently task.

Aside from a kind of famous photosensitizer, carbon dots (CDs) have also been widely developed as metal-free photocatalyst, endowing them with the potential to become light-responsive oxidase-like nanozymes(Ren et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2018). Moreover, the CDs derived from the precursors with abundant electron donating and drawing groups are susceptible to intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), which is beneficial for the improvement of their catalytic capabilities. Herein, by adopting julolidine, 8-hydroxyjulolidine and citric acid as precursors, we have synthesized a kind of light-response CDs and used them as oxidase-like nanozyme to determine TAC in a colorimetric system. Under the light illumination, the developed CDs can catalyze the oxygen to oxidize colorimetric substrate (3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine, TMB) with a Michaelis constant as low as 0.22 mM, due to the production of hydroxyl radical. On the contrary, the oxidation of TMB is restricted when antioxidants are presented (Scheme 1 ). Based on this, this CDs-based assay system can realize the detection of antioxidants like glutathione (GSH), l-ascorbic acid (AA) and l-cysteine (Cys). Moreover, by expressing as AA equivalent antioxidant capacity, the TAC in fruits has also been obtained with satisfied results.

TMB, citric acid and 8-Hydroxyjulolidine were provided by Aladdin Chemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Julolidine and AA were purchased from J&K Scientific. Ethanol and dichloromethane were provided by Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. GSH and Cys was bought from Shanghai Energy Chemical and Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China) Co., Ltd., respectively. Three kinds of fruits were purchased from local supermarkets. Light conditions were provided by the photochemical reaction chamber (CEL-LB70). The ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm, Milli-Q) was applied during preparing all aqueous solutions.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was collected on a Tecnai G2 transmission electron microscope (FEI Corporation, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectra was measured by FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet iS50). UV − vis absorption spectra analysis was carried out by a UV − vis spectrophotometer (Agilent 8453, Agilent Technologies Ltd.). X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi) was used to collect X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a F-7100 fluorometer (Hitachi High-Technologies Corporation).

A typical microwave hydrothermal method was adopted for preparing CDs. In brief, the precursors including julolidine (17.3 mg), citric acid (38.4 mg), and 8-hydroxyjulolidine (18.9 mg) were dissolved in ethanol absolute solution (4 mL). The above mixture was placed into microwave reactor. After 2 h of heating (160 °C), the as-obtained solution was collected and purified by silica column chromatography. The eluent was prepared by mixing dichloromethane and ethanol in a volume ratio of 10:1. After rotatory evaporation, we obtained the CDs, which was deposited at room temperature for further use.

TMB, a typical oxidase substrate, was selected for probing oxidase-like catalytic activity of CDs. The acetic acid/sodium acetate buffer solution was used in this work. 1 mL of buffer solution (pH 3.5, 0.1 M) containing the as-prepared CDs (75 μg mL−1) and TMB (1 mM) were prepared, which was then irradiated by a filter-equipped Xe lamp (130 mW cm−2, 400 nm) for 20 min. During this light illumination process, the oxygen dissolved in the solution was activated to oxidize TMB. Finally, we collected UV–vis absorption spectra. Optimization experiments were performed using similar procedure. pH optimization tests were performed in a range from 3 to 6 with an interval of 0.5, while the optimization test of CDs concentration was executed from 25 to 125 μg mL−1 (Concentration interval is 25 μg mL−1). The optimization of irradiation time was tested from 10 to 60 min (Time interval is 10 min).

For steady-state kinetics assay, the tests were performed using same buffer solutions (1 mL) with constant CDs concentration and a variety of TMB concentrations (from 0.2 to 1.2 mM). After 20 mins of light illumination, we collected their absorbance values at 652 nm. The kinetics constants including maximal reaction velocity (Vmax) and Michaelis constant (Km) was calculated according to the Michaelis-Menten equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic mechanism for photo-responsive oxidase-like activity
ce:section.ce:para: By the introduction of various scavengers (tryptophan, tertiary butyl alcohol, catalase, superoxide dismutase and ethylenediaminetetraacetate, EDTA), the potential intermediates were detected for probing the photo-responsive catalytic mechanism of CDs. Different scavengers were mixed with as-mentioned buffer solution (1 mL), CDs (75 μg mL−1) and TMB (1 mM), respectively. The final concentrations of these scavengers were 2.5 mg mL−1, 50 mM, and 1 mg mL−1, 300 U mL−1 and 5 mM, respectively. After light illumination for 20 min, the absorbance values (652 nm) of these samples were recorded. The TMB oxidation degrees were the absorbance value ratios of samples with and without scavengers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of antioxidants and TAC in fruit
ce:section.ce:para: According to previous reports (Guo et al., 2021; Han et al., 2022), the AA, GSH and Cys were selected as antioxidants, which were used to perform the colorimetric assay. Typically, we prepared 1 mL of mixed solution, which contained as-mentioned buffer solution, CDs (75 μg mL−1) and TMB (1 mM) as well as antioxidants with a variety of concentrations at room temperature. The concentration range started from 0 to 50 μM for these three antioxidants. After that, the samples were immediately irradiated, and their absorbance values (652 nm) were recorded. Such procedure was also used to investigate the selectivity of above-mentioned assay by using various potential interference factors (50 μM) to replace antioxidant. The interference factors including glycine (Gly), phenylalanine (Phe), tryptophan (Try), tyrosine (Tyr), histidine (His), leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), threonine (Thr), and metal ions (K+, Na+, Mg2+) were selected according to previous reports(Sun et al., 2022; Tian et al., 2020).

As to TAC assay of fruits, the tomatoes, kiwis, and oranges purchased from local market were selected as models. After juicing, centrifugation and filtration and attenuation processes, the samples (10 μL) were injected to the buffer solution with CDs (75 μg mL−1) and TMB (1 mM) to form a mixture solution (1 mL). The following process was same as previously described. The TACs were expressed as molar equivalent of AA. To confirm the assay accuracy, the standard addition method was also applied. In these tests, the mixture solution additionally contained AA (10 μM), and the recoveries were calculated.

The CDs were prepared using julolidine, citric acid and 8-hydroxyjulolidine as precursors, coupling with a microwave-assisted hydrothermal approach. The TEM analysis discloses that the as-prepared CDs possess a quasi-spherical structure with a high dispersity (Fig. 1 a). The particle size falls within the range of 2.5 to 4.5 nm, and the average diameter is about 3.5 nm (Fig. 1b). FTIR and XPS spectra analyses were performed to probe the functional groups on CD surfaces. The peaks at 3439 and 2925 cm−1 are indexed to NH/OH and CH stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. 1c). The peaks at 1735, 1630 1385 and 1081 cm−1 are corresponded to CO, CC, CN and COC bonds, respectively(Geng et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2020). The wide XPS spectrum shows that these CDs are dominated by C, N, O elements, while the corresponding content is 6.55 %, 85.04 %, 8.41 %, respectively (Fig. 1d). Moreover, the deconvolution of C1s spectra shows three peaks at 284.0, 284.8 and 285.50 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN, and CO groups, respectively (Fig. 1e). Similarly, N1s peak can be divided into the characteristic peaks of CN, CN and NH bond (394.7, 395.5 and 397.6 eV), while the O1s peaks can be decomposed to four carbon bonds, including COOH/CO, C-OH, OCO and CO bonds (527.3, 528.3, 529.2, and 530.2 eV, Fig. 2 f). The results of XPS are matched well with that of FTIR, indicating that hydroxyl, carboxyl and amide groups are involved in the as-prepared CDs. The presence of these electron donors and electron acceptors can facilitate the intramolecular charge transfer effect, which is conducive to the generation of catalytic activity (Epelde-Elezcano et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017). To probe the photo-responsive capability, the UV–vis absorption curve has also been recorded in Fig. 1f. The result reveals that CDs have large absorption in the visible region. Moreover, the maximum absorption wavelength is far from that of TMB (570 vs 652 nm), suggesting that the CDs will not cause any optical interferences during the colorimetric assay. Under an excitation wavelength of 530 nm, the fluorescence spectrum discloses an emission peak at 600 nm, further confirming the successful preparation of CDs.

By taking advantage of TMB as substrate, the light-response oxidase-like activity of CDs was investigated. No obvious absorption peak can be found in the UV–vis spectroscopy of TMB solution after the irradiation for 20 min, and the solution basically presents colorless (Fig. 2a). Under the same irradiation condition, the TMB and CDs co-existed solution exhibits a blue color and displays a strong absorption peak (652 nm) in the UV–vis spectroscopy. Since the pure CDs solution has not showed similar changes under light irradiation, these changes can be attributed to the catalytic oxidation of TMB by CDs. Furthermore, these changes have not occurred when the TMB and CDs co-existed solution has not underwent light irradiation, confirming that the oxidization capability of CDs is indeed associated with light stimulation. Moreover, we investigated the controllability of oxidase-like activity of CDs by successively imposing and cancelling light stimulation. The result showed that the oxidase-like catalytic ability of CDs was activated immediately by light stimulation and disappeared rapidly after cancelling light stimulation during eight test cycles (Fig. 2b). Such staircase-like behavior powerfully demonstrates that the oxidase-like activity is optically controlled. Experimental conditions including pH, CDs concentration and irradiation time were optimized for achieving the best oxidase-like activity. The optimal pH value and CDs concentration are 3.5 and 75 μg mL−1, respectively. After 20 min of irradiation, the color change of the solution is easily to capture by naked eye. Therefore, the optimal irradiation time was selected as 20 min form a comprehensive consideration of detection time and visualization (Fig. S1). With these optimal conditions in hands, the steady-state kinetic assay was then performed for obtaining Michaelis-Menten curve and Lineweaver-Burk plot (Fig. 2c, d). The Km and Vmax are corresponded to the slope and intercept of curve in Fig. 2d, which is 0.22 mM and 2.74 × 10-8 M s−1, respectively. Even compared to the metal-involved oxidase-like nanozyme(Han et al., 2022), the CDs show a comparable Vmax value but a lower Km value. The lower Km manifests a better affinity between CDs and the substrate, endowing CDs with better compatibility with TMB-involved colorimetric assay.

The intermediates during the catalytic process of CDs under the light irradiation were detected by the addition of scavengers. As the photocatalytic oxidation process would involve hydroxyl radicals, superoxide anion radical, singlet oxygen, hydrogen peroxide and photo-generated holes, thus the corresponding scavengers were selected, including tertiary butyl alcohol (TBA), superoxide dismutase (SOD), tryptophan (Try), catalase and EDTA(Chen et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2019; Ni et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the TMB oxidation degree displays a significant decrease when the introduction of tertiary butyl alcohol, while other scavengers have not displayed obvious influences towards photo-catalytic oxidation process, reflecting the possibility to generate hydroxyl radical. By using DMPO as a trapping probe, we also performed the electron paramagnetic resonance (ERP) spectroscopy. We observed a 1:2:2:1 characterized hydroxyl radical four-line spectrum with the irradiation of CDs solution for 20 min (Fig. S2), which further confirmed that the hydroxyl radical was the main intermediate. Moreover, we probe the dependency between this photocatalytic oxidation process and oxygen by collecting the UV–vis absorption spectra after completing reaction. We can observe that peak intensity (652 nm) sharply reduces when oxygen missing (Fig. 3b), reflecting that oxygen is an important precursor for this photocatalytic oxidation process. Therefore, we can deduce that CDs catalyze the dissolved oxygen to generate hydroxyl radical for oxidizing TMB as well as manifesting oxidase-like activity under light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of antioxidant activity of AA, GSH and Cys
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the competitive effect, the oxidation process of TMB catalyzed by CDs will be inhibited by the addition of antioxidant. Therefore, the CDs-coupled TMB system was applied to assay three antioxidants, including AA, GSH and Cys. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the characteristic peak of oxidized TMB gradually declined when increasing AA concentration. Furthermore, the AA concentration in the range from 1 to 30 μM exhibited an ideal linear correlation with the loss of the absorption value at 652 nm (R2 = 0.9968). The detection limit was 1.53 μM (Fig. 4b). Similar changes in the characteristic absorption peak of oxidized TMB have been observed with the addition of GSH and Cys with a variety of concentrations (Fig. S3, S4). For GSH and Cys, the linear detection ranges were 1–30 and 2–10 μM (R2 = 0.9903, 0.9986), while detection limits were 2.00 and 0.97 μM, respectively. All of these results powerfully demonstrate that the CDs-coupled TMB system can detect these antioxidants with satisfied sensitivity. To exclude the interference of the common metal ions and amino acids in fruits, their effects on the colorimetric assay of AA were probed. The K+, Na+ and Mg2+ were selected as the tested metal ions, while the selected amino acids contained glycine, phenylalanine, tryptophan, tyrosine, histidine, leucine, lysine and threonine. As depicted in Fig. 4c, neither of metal ions and amino acids showed obvious interferences on the AA detection, suggesting the good selectivity of this CDs-coupled TMB colorimetric assay system. As well known, the real antioxidant activity corresponds to the synergic and redox interactions of all molecules existed in food. However, the CDs could only produce hydroxyl radical under light irradiation. In this case, we should note that the antioxidant activity here was designated as the hydroxyl radical scavenging ability of antioxidant.

To probe the practicability of the developed CDs-coupled TMB colorimetric assay system, we assessed the TACs of three different fruits. Since TAC is defined by millimolar equivalents of AA, the as-established standard curve for AA detection was applied. The kiwi fruit possesses the highest TAC among all tested fruits (Fig. 4d, S5), which is equal to 11.40 ± 0.77 μM of AA. Furthermore, the TAC of orange and tomato is approximate, corresponding to 11.00 ± 0.50 and 7.56 ± 0.89 μM of AA. These results will be beneficial for the planning of daily diet. To verify the accuracy and reliability of this assay system, the standard addition method was also applied (Table 1 ). After deducting the TAC contents detected by standard curve method, the recovery efficiencies of AA in these three fruits are 104.60 ± 3.20 %, 94.90 ± 3.05 % and 102.70 ± 5.00 %. Thus, our proposed assay system could become an effective tool for acquiring the TAC in fruits.

In conclusion, we have exploited a sort of CDs by using the precursors with abundant electron donating and drawing groups, which possessed a photo-controllable oxidase-like activity. The scavenger experiment revealed that the CDs might generate hydroxyl radical during catalytic oxidation reaction. Furthermore, CDs exhibited a desirable affinity to TMB with a Km as low as 0.22 mM. Thanks to oxidase-like activity and good compatibility with TMB, the CDs were successfully coupled with TMB to construct a colorimetric sensor, which realized the detection of antioxidants and TACs of fruits with high sensitivity and accuracy. This works not only presents a practical strategy for TAC assessment, but also opens up the channel between photo-responsive carbon dots and oxidase-like nanozymes.

Jinquan Li: Methodology, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Investigation. Yun Zhou: Methodology, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Investigation. Yue Xiao: Conceptualization. Shuo Cai: Visualization. Changsheng Huang: Visualization. Shuo Guo: Visualization. Yuanqiang Sun: Project administration. Rong-Bin Song: Writing – review & editing. Zhaohui Li: Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21974125, 22174131), Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Henan Province (22TRTSTHN002), Key Project of Scientific and Technological in Henan Province (212102311059), the 111 Project of Henan Province (CXJD2021001), National 111 Project of China (D20003) and state Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2108).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.134749.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
